text: The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, entering the mesentery between the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is traced up to the confluence with the ileocolic vein, revealing the branching pattern. Lymphatics at the ileomesenteric junctions are visibly resected.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, entering the mesentery between the ileocolic and superior mesenteric vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is traced up to the confluence with the ileocolic vein, revealing the ideal branches and ensuring complete resection of lymphatics at the ileomesenteric junctions.
The surgeon identifies the correct dissection plane between the retroperitoneum and the ileocolic region, with the duodenum visible from a ventral and inferior perspective.
The surgeon ensures the correct dissection plane between the retroperitoneum and the ileocolic region by visualizing the duodenum from a ventral and inferior perspective.
The surgeon seals the ileocolic pedicle using a radiofrequency device, without the use of clips or other suture devices, ensuring hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon seals the ileocolic pedicle using a radiofrequency device, without the use of clips or other suture devices, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.

The surgeon mobilizes the dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon and the mesenteric root, preserving the lateral colonic attachments. The dissection proceeds from the distal mesoilium in a medial to lateral fashion, carefully cleaving the right colon from the right peritoneum from bottom to top over the right colic flexure to avoid ureter injury.

The surgeon mobilizes the dorsal aspect of the ascending mesocolon and the mesenteric root, preserving the lateral colonic attachments. The dissection proceeds from the distal mesoilium in a medial to lateral direction. The right colon is cleaved from the right peritoneal side, moving from bottom to top over the right colic flexure to avoid ureter injury.

The surgeon dissects around the duodenum and pancreas, isolating and sealing the right branch of the metacolic vessels at their confluence with the left branch. The gastrocolic ligament is divided, reaching the hepatic flexure, while the transverse mesocolon is separated from the dorsal mesogastrium. The marginal artery is isolated and sealed as the final vascular step.

The surgeon isolates and seals the right branch of the metacolic vessels at their confluence with the left branch during the dissection of the duodenum and pancreas. The division of the gastrocolic ligament continues until reaching the hepatic flexure, while the transverse mesocolon is separated from the dorsal mesogastrium. The marginal artery is isolated and sealed, marking the final vascular step of the procedure.
The surgeon uses cyanine green enhanced fluorescence to visually assess bowel perfusion in the distal ileum and transverse colon, determining the optimal transection line based on perfusion quality.

The surgeon uses cyanine green enhanced fluorescence to visually assess bowel perfusion in the distal ileum and transverse colon, determining the appropriate transection line based on the perfusion results.

Following specimen resection, the surgeon performs a primary intracorporeal side-to-side stapled isoperistaltic anastomosis between the distal ileum and transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double-layer closure of the enterotomy.

Following specimen resection, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the distal ileum and the transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double-layer closure of the enterotomy.
